answer:
Both Cynewulf's Andreas (cynewulf-andreas-07b573, chapter 1) and Iqbal's Bang-E-Dara (across all three parts) grapple with the relationship between human action and the divine, but from starkly different theological stances.

**Andreas: Human action as unconditional obedience to a commanding God**

In Andreas (chapter 1), the divine-human relationship is structured as a hierarchy of command and obedience. God issues direct, unambiguous directives and Andrew (Andreas) must execute them. The poem opens by establishing that the twelve apostles' fates were "laid out as the Lord Himself, / Heaven's High King, handed them their fates" (paragraph p-868413). Andrew's mission to Mermedonia is entirely God-initiated: "Then the Lord of Hosts spoke his holy word, / commanding Andrew with a clear voice" (p-4d1e78). When Andrew demurs — asking how he could possibly make the journey — God rebukes him: "Ah, my good Andrew, why are you so slow, / your faith so feeble, your feet so unwilling?" (p-c4d450). Human action in Andreas is thus validated only insofar as it fulfills divine command. Andrew's courage during his torture is repeatedly sustained by divine reassurance: God appears in the prison cell, heals Andrew's wounds miraculously, and finally acts directly — the flood, the blooming groves appearing on the blood-stained ground — to vindicate Andrew's suffering. The human hero's role is one of suffering, persevering, and obeying; God supplies the miraculous power. The relationship is never dialogic in any adversarial sense: Andrew does not challenge God's logic but only his own capacity. When the Mermedonians finally convert, they acclaim: "Now it is seen that the true Maker, / the King of all creatures, wields with His crafts" (p-839aab). Human transformation is the product of divine intervention channeled through the saint's obedient action.

**Bang-E-Dara: Human action as co-creative, dialogic, and historically urgent**

Iqbal's approach in Bang-E-Dara is far more dialectical. Human beings are not passive conduits for divine commands but active co-agents in realizing the divine plan — and when they fail to act, the relationship can be interrogated and even indicted.

In Part 3, chapter 20 (Shikwa / The Complaint), Iqbal stages the boldest possible challenge: the Muslim community directly accuses God of ingratitude. The poem rehearses a history of Muslim martial and spiritual action on God's behalf — "It was we, and we alone, who were Your champions in the fight... We called the prayer in Europe's churches, burning with Your light" — and then demands to know why divine favor has been withdrawn. The human community's historical agency (jihad, prayer, the spread of monotheism) is presented as the very ground on which divine reciprocity is owed. This is the opposite of Andreas's docile obedience: Iqbal's speaker risks "the penalty of pain" (Part 1, ch. 12, p. 1) in speaking at all, asserting that "Forgive my tongue, but God, I have a grievance to declare."

In Part 1, chapter 37 (Sarguzasht-e-Adam / The Saga of Man), the human being's defining characteristic is restless striving — "With such a restless nature, loving change, was I endowed" — that drives history itself: from Sinai to the Cross to the cave of Hira, from Greek philosophy to the discovery of natural law. Here human action is autonomous and exploratory, not commanded, and the divine is the destination only dimly glimpsed at the journey's end: "when my eye that worships form at last was opened wide, / I found that in my own heart's house He did reside."

In Part 3, chapter 12 (Insaan / The Human Being), Iqbal identifies the definitive separation of humanity from passive nature: "Whatever exists in the world is accustomed to submission; / Every power of the Human Being is aflame with striving." Where a pine tree has no longing, human beings can "change the very form of the garden." This is an anthropology of creative agency, not obedient service.

In Part 3, chapter 13 (Khitab ba Jawanan-e-Islam / Address to Muslim Youth), historical failure is diagnosed as the failure of action: "you are all talk, they were all action; you are static, they were ever-moving." The divine gift is not rescinded — the Muslim inheritance is intact — but it is only activated by human striving.

**The core contrast**

In Andreas (chapter 1), the divine sets the agenda and provides miraculous power; human action is meritorious obedience that earns divine protection. The poem's theology is one of grace through perseverance under command. In Bang-E-Dara, the divine-human axis is more mutual: God needs human champions (Shikwa, Part 3 ch. 20), humans need divine inspiration, and the relationship is subject to negotiation, complaint, and renewal. Iqbal's human being strives autonomously (Sarguzasht-e-Adam, Part 1 ch. 37; Insaan, Part 3 ch. 12), while Cynewulf's Andrew obeys and suffers, finding glory through absolute fidelity to a commanding Lord.

tool_calls.result_summary: Huzoor Risalat Maab Mein: Iqbal presents Muslim community's blood (action/sacrifice) as the one gift heaven lacks — human action has irreplaceable value even for the divine realm
